name: Kismet
short_description: An expressive robotic head built at MIT in the late 1990s to engage people in face-to-face social and emotional exchange.
long_description: Kismet is a disembodied robot head developed by Cynthia Breazeal as her doctoral work under Rodney Brooks at MIT, widely regarded as a founding artifact of social robotics. Using cameras, microphones tuned to infant-directed speech, and a synthetic-nervous-system control architecture, it perceives a human partner and responds with legible affective displays through motorized ears, eyebrows, eyelids, lips, jaw, and neck, plus expressive vocal babble synthesized with DECtalk. Modeled on infant-caregiver interaction, Kismet maintained an internal motivation-and-emotion state that drove turn-taking and proto-conversational engagement rather than completing any external task. It now resides in the MIT Museum.

images.caption: Kismet (1993-2000) on display at the MIT Museum, Cambridge, Massachusetts.
images.alt_text: A robotic head with large round camera eyes, expressive pink rubber lips, mobile eyebrows, and pointed ears mounted on a metal neck, displayed in a museum case.

sources.title: Kismet (robot)

sources.title: Kismet - The First Social Robot

sources.title: Kismet - ROBOTS: Your Guide to the World of Robotics

interactions.title: Scold it and watch its face fall
interactions.description: Speak to Kismet in a sharp, prohibiting tone and its features sag into a crestfallen look; switch to warm praise and it brightens. It reads the affect in your voice, not your words, sorting speech into approval, prohibition, attention, comfort, or neutral.

interactions.title: Babble back and forth like an infant
interactions.description: Kismet holds a proto-conversation in nonsense phonemes synthesized through DECtalk, leaning in and pausing with the right gaze and posture so you instinctively take your turn. Naive visitors fall into the rhythm and the back-and-forth smooths out within minutes.

interactions.title: Overwhelm it and it shuts down to recover
interactions.description: Wave a stuffed toy too fast and too close and Kismet first looks annoyed, then closes its eyes and 'goes to sleep' to escape the flood of stimulation. Back off and it calms, settling back toward a comfortable middle of engagement.

interactions.title: Smile at it and it smiles back
interactions.description: Stand in front of Kismet at the MIT Museum and offer a smile or a frown; its rubber lips, brows, and ears arrange into a matching expression as its camera eyes track your face around the case.

interactions.title: A child decides the robot doesn't like her
interactions.description: In Sherry Turkle's MIT fieldwork, twelve-year-old Estelle kept playing until Kismet glitched and went quiet; she read the silence as rejection, grew sullen, and withdrew, convinced the robot simply didn't like her.

interactions.title: Coax a withdrawn robot back into play
interactions.description: When Kismet droops into an understimulated, lonely-looking state, lean in and address it gently; it perceives the renewed attention and re-engages, so a quiet visitor can effectively nurse it out of a sulk. Grounded in its motivation-driven seeking of social contact and its comfort-tone recognition.

interactions.title: Pace a conversation by reading its mood
interactions.description: Treat its expressions as a feedback meter: slow down when its brows knit toward overwhelm, add energy when it looks bored, and let its face teach you the tempo it can handle. Grounded in the legible affect displays and homeostatic regulation it already runs.
